title: PDF attachments are corrupted after upload
body: When customers upload PDF files to their tasks, the files come through broken. You can't open them — the PDF viewer says the file is damaged. This is happening for multiple customers. We use the file attachment feature heavily so this is really impacting our workflow.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for reporting this — corrupted file uploads are definitely something we want to get to the bottom of quickly.

A few things to start narrowing this down: **Did PDF uploads work correctly before, and if so, when did you first notice the corruption?** Also, is this happening with *every* PDF you upload, or only certain ones (e.g., larger files, scanned documents, PDFs with forms)? Finally, when you say the file is damaged — are you downloading the file back from TaskFlow and then trying to open it, or is the preview within TaskFlow itself broken?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Yes it worked fine before, this started maybe a week ago? I don't know the exact date. It's not every single PDF but it's a LOT of them. Some seem fine but most are broken. We download them and try to open them and they're just corrupted. Look, our support team relies on this daily and we're having to email files around as a workaround right now. Can you just fix it?

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
I hear you — I know this is blocking your team's daily work and we want to get it resolved. Let me ask something that will help us zero in on the cause:

For the PDFs that come through corrupted vs. the ones that work fine, have you noticed any pattern around **file size**? For example, do smaller PDFs tend to work while larger ones break? If you can, pick one corrupted file and one that worked, and compare their sizes — even rough numbers like "the broken one was 5MB, the working one was 200KB" would be very helpful.

Also, when you download a corrupted file from TaskFlow, does the **downloaded file size** look roughly the same as the original, or is it noticeably smaller/larger?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: I don't know the exact sizes off the top of my head, but now that you mention it... yeah, the ones that work might be smaller ones. Like quick one-page things. The ones that are broken tend to be bigger reports, multi-page stuff. I haven't compared the download sizes, I just know they won't open. Can you check your end? You should be able to see the uploads in your system.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
I completely understand the urgency, and the pattern you've noticed — small PDFs working, larger ones breaking — is actually a really helpful clue. It points us toward a specific class of bug.

Could you do one quick test for me? Pick one corrupted PDF where you still have the original file on your computer:

1. Right-click the **original file** on your computer and check its size (e.g., Properties on Windows, Get Info on Mac).
2. Download that same file from TaskFlow and check **the downloaded copy's** size the same way.
3. Tell me both numbers.

This will tell us whether the file is being **cut short** during upload (truncation) vs. **scrambled** in some other way — and that distinction points to very different fixes. It should only take a minute and it'll save us a lot of back-and-forth.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Ugh, fine. I just checked one. The original is 2.4MB and the downloaded one from TaskFlow is... also about 2.4MB actually. So it's not smaller or anything, it's just broken. The file is all there size-wise but it won't open. Look I've already spent way too much time on this, can someone on your engineering team just look at what changed recently? This was working fine like a week ago and then it wasn't.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"PDF attachments silently corrupted during upload/download — size preserved, content damaged (regression, ~1 week)","problem":"PDF files uploaded to task attachments are being corrupted. When customers download the files, PDF viewers report them as damaged. The file size is preserved (a 2.4MB original downloads as ~2.4MB), ruling out truncation. The issue disproportionately affects larger, multi-page PDFs while smaller single-page PDFs tend to survive intact. Multiple customers are affected. This is a regression that began approximately one week ago; the feature was previously working correctly.","root_cause_hypothesis":"Size-preserved corruption strongly suggests a binary-to-text encoding issue introduced in a recent change. Most likely: a component in the upload or download pipeline is now treating the binary PDF stream as text and applying a character encoding transformation (e.g., UTF-8 sanitization, BOM insertion, or line-ending normalization). This would corrupt multi-byte sequences in the binary data while leaving simpler/smaller files less visibly damaged. Alternatively, a new middleware layer (content filter, virus scanner, or CDN edge processing) may be modifying the binary stream. A chunked upload reassembly bug where chunks are reordered is also possible but less likely given that some files work fine regardless of the corruption.","reproduction_steps":["Prepare a multi-page PDF file (>1MB, e.g., a report or scanned document)","Upload the PDF as a task attachment in TaskFlow","Download the attachment from TaskFlow","Attempt to open the downloaded file — it should fail with a 'damaged/corrupted' error","Compare the original and downloaded file sizes (expect them to match)","Run a hex diff between original and downloaded copies to identify the corruption pattern"],"environment":"Affects multiple customers across unknown browsers/OS. The feature is used heavily by support teams. Regression window is approximately 1 week prior to report (late March / early April 2026).","severity":"high","impact":"Multiple customers' daily workflows are blocked. Support teams that rely on PDF attachments are forced to use email as a workaround, degrading productivity and creating process fragmentation.","recommended_fix":"1. Identify all deployments/config changes to the file upload/download pipeline from the past 1-2 weeks. 2. Hex-diff an original PDF against its downloaded copy to characterize the corruption pattern (look for byte substitutions consistent with encoding conversion, line-ending changes, or chunk boundary artifacts). 3. Inspect the upload and download code paths for any text-mode stream handling, encoding transforms, or new middleware. 4. Check for changes to storage layer configuration, CDN settings, or reverse proxy rules that might alter binary content. 5. Verify Content-Type headers are set to application/octet-stream or application/pdf (not text/*) throughout the pipeline.","proposed_test_case":"Upload PDFs of varying sizes (100KB, 1MB, 5MB, 10MB) and binary complexity. After download, perform a byte-for-byte comparison (e.g., sha256sum) between original and downloaded files. All checksums must match. Include PDFs with embedded images, forms, and multi-byte unicode text to cover encoding edge cases.","information_gaps":["Exact date the regression was introduced (reporter said 'about a week ago' — server-side deploy logs will pinpoint this)","Whether non-PDF binary files (images, DOCX, ZIP) are also affected (engineering can test this to confirm whether the bug is PDF-specific or affects all binary uploads)","Exact corruption pattern visible in a hex diff (engineering will obtain this during investigation)","Which specific browsers/clients are in use (unlikely to matter if the corruption is server-side, as suggested by multiple customers being affected)"]}
